speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Verona, for a while I take my leave

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: To see my friends in Padua, but of all

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: My best belovèd and approvèd friend,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Hortensio. And I trow this is his house.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Knock, sir? Whom should I knock? Is there

speaker: GRUMIO
text: any man has rebused your Worship?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Knock you here, sir? Why, sir, what am I, sir,

speaker: GRUMIO
text: that I should knock you here, sir?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And rap me well, or I’ll knock your knave’s pate.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: My master is grown quarrelsome. I should knock you first,

speaker: GRUMIO
text: And then I know after who comes by the worst.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Will it not be?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Faith, sirrah, an you’ll not knock, I’ll ring it.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I’ll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Help, mistress, help! My master is mad.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: villain.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: How now, what’s the matter? My old

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: friend Grumio and my good friend Petruchio? How

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: do you all at Verona?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Con tutto il cuore ben trovato, may I say.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Alia nostra casa ben venuto, molto

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: honorato signor mio Petruchio.—Rise, Grumio,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: rise. We will compound this quarrel.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Nay, ’tis no matter, sir, what he ’leges in

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Latin. If this be not a lawful cause for me to leave

speaker: GRUMIO
text: his service—look you, sir: he bid me knock him

speaker: GRUMIO
text: and rap him soundly, sir. Well, was it fit for a

speaker: GRUMIO
text: servant to use his master so, being perhaps, for

speaker: GRUMIO
text: aught I see, two-and-thirty, a pip out?

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Whom, would to God, I had well knocked at first,

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Then had not Grumio come by the worst.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: A senseless villain, good Hortensio.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I bade the rascal knock upon your gate

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And could not get him for my heart to do it.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Knock at the gate? O, heavens, spake you not

speaker: GRUMIO
text: these words plain: “Sirrah, knock me here, rap me

speaker: GRUMIO
text: here, knock me well, and knock me soundly”? And

speaker: GRUMIO
text: come you now with “knocking at the gate”?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Sirrah, begone, or talk not, I advise you.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Petruchio, patience. I am Grumio’s pledge.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Why, this’ a heavy chance ’twixt him and you,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Your ancient, trusty, pleasant servant Grumio.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And tell me now, sweet friend, what happy gale

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Blows you to Padua here from old Verona?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Such wind as scatters young men through the world

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: To seek their fortunes farther than at home,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Where small experience grows. But in a few,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me:

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Antonio, my father, is deceased,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And I have thrust myself into this maze,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Happily to wive and thrive, as best I may.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Crowns in my purse I have and goods at home,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And so am come abroad to see the world.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Petruchio, shall I then come roundly to thee

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And wish thee to a shrewd ill-favored wife?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Thou ’dst thank me but a little for my counsel—

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And yet I’ll promise thee she shall be rich,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And very rich. But thou ’rt too much my friend,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And I’ll not wish thee to her.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Signior Hortensio, ’twixt such friends as we

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Few words suffice. And therefore, if thou know

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: One rich enough to be Petruchio’s wife

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: (As wealth is burden of my wooing dance),

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Be she as foul as was Florentius’ love,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As old as Sibyl, and as curst and shrewd

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As Socrates’ Xanthippe, or a worse,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: She moves me not, or not removes at least

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Affection’s edge in me, were she as rough

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As are the swelling Adriatic seas.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I come to wive it wealthily in Padua;

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If wealthily, then happily in Padua.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Nay, look you, sir, he tells you

speaker: GRUMIO
text: flatly what his mind is. Why, give him gold enough

speaker: GRUMIO
text: and marry him to a puppet or an aglet-baby, or an

speaker: GRUMIO
text: old trot with ne’er a tooth in her head, though she

speaker: GRUMIO
text: have as many diseases as two-and-fifty horses. Why,

speaker: GRUMIO
text: nothing comes amiss, so money comes withal.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Petruchio, since we are stepped thus far in,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I will continue that I broached in jest.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I can, Petruchio, help thee to a wife

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: With wealth enough, and young and beauteous,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Brought up as best becomes a gentlewoman.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Her only fault, and that is faults enough,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Is that she is intolerable curst,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And shrewd, and froward, so beyond all measure

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: That, were my state far worser than it is,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I would not wed her for a mine of gold.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Hortensio, peace. Thou know’st not gold’s effect.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Tell me her father’s name, and ’tis enough;

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For I will board her, though she chide as loud

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Her father is Baptista Minola,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: An affable and courteous gentleman.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Her name is Katherina Minola,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Renowned in Padua for her scolding tongue.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I know her father, though I know not her,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And he knew my deceasèd father well.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And therefore let me be thus bold with you

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: To give you over at this first encounter—

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Unless you will accompany me thither.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: I pray you, sir, let him go while

speaker: GRUMIO
text: the humor lasts. O’ my word, an she knew him as

speaker: GRUMIO
text: well as I do, she would think scolding would do little

speaker: GRUMIO
text: good upon him. She may perhaps call him half a

speaker: GRUMIO
text: score knaves or so. Why, that’s nothing; an he begin

speaker: GRUMIO
text: once, he’ll rail in his rope tricks. I’ll tell you what,

speaker: GRUMIO
text: sir, an she stand him but a little, he will throw a

speaker: GRUMIO
text: figure in her face and so disfigure her with it that

speaker: GRUMIO
text: she shall have no more eyes to see withal than a cat.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: You know him not, sir.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Tarry, Petruchio. I must go with thee,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: For in Baptista’s keep my treasure is.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: He hath the jewel of my life in hold,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: His youngest daughter, beautiful Bianca,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And her withholds from me and other more,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Suitors to her and rivals in my love,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Supposing it a thing impossible,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: For those defects I have before rehearsed,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: That ever Katherina will be wooed.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Therefore this order hath Baptista ta’en,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: That none shall have access unto Bianca

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Till Katherine the curst have got a husband.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: “Katherine the curst,”

speaker: GRUMIO
text: A title for a maid, of all titles the worst.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Now shall my friend Petruchio do me grace

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And offer me disguised in sober robes

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: To old Baptista as a schoolmaster

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Well seen in music, to instruct Bianca,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: That so I may, by this device at least,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Have leave and leisure to make love to her

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And unsuspected court her by herself.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Here’s no knavery! See, to beguile the old

speaker: GRUMIO
text: folks, how the young folks lay their heads together!

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Master, master, look about you. Who goes there, ha?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Peace, Grumio, it is the rival of my love.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Petruchio, stand by awhile.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: A proper stripling, and an amorous.

speaker: GREMIO
text: O, very well, I have perused the note.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Hark you, sir, I’ll have them very fairly bound,

speaker: GREMIO
text: All books of love. See that at any hand,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And see you read no other lectures to her.

speaker: GREMIO
text: You understand me. Over and beside

speaker: GREMIO
text: Signior Baptista’s liberality,

speaker: GREMIO
text: I’ll mend it with a largess. Take your paper too.

speaker: GREMIO
text: And let me have them very well perfumed,

speaker: GREMIO
text: For she is sweeter than perfume itself

speaker: GREMIO
text: To whom they go to. What will you read to her?

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: Whate’er I read to her, I’ll plead for you

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: As for my patron, stand you so assured,

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: As firmly as yourself were still in place,

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: Yea, and perhaps with more successful words

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: Than you—unless you were a scholar, sir.

speaker: GREMIO
text: O this learning, what a thing it is!

speaker: GRUMIO
text: O this woodcock, what an ass it is!

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Peace, sirrah.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Grumio, mum.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: God save you, Signior Gremio.

speaker: GREMIO
text: And you are well met, Signior Hortensio.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Trow you whither I am going? To Baptista Minola.

speaker: GREMIO
text: I promised to enquire carefully

speaker: GREMIO
text: About a schoolmaster for the fair Bianca,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And by good fortune I have lighted well

speaker: GREMIO
text: On this young man, for learning and behavior

speaker: GREMIO
text: Fit for her turn, well read in poetry

speaker: GREMIO
text: And other books—good ones, I warrant you.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: ’Tis well. And I have met a gentleman

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Hath promised me to help me to another,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: A fine musician to instruct our mistress.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: So shall I no whit be behind in duty

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: To fair Bianca, so beloved of me.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Beloved of me, and that my deeds shall prove.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: And that his bags shall prove.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Gremio, ’tis now no time to vent our love.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Listen to me, and if you speak me fair

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I’ll tell you news indifferent good for either.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Here is a gentleman whom by chance I met,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Upon agreement from us to his liking,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Will undertake to woo curst Katherine,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Yea, and to marry her, if her dowry please.

speaker: GREMIO
text: So said, so done, is well.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I know she is an irksome, brawling scold.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.

speaker: GREMIO
text: No? Sayst me so, friend? What countryman?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Born in Verona, old Antonio’s son.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: My father dead, my fortune lives for me,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And I do hope good days and long to see.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Oh, sir, such a life with such a wife were strange.

speaker: GREMIO
text: But if you have a stomach, to ’t, i’ God’s name!

speaker: GREMIO
text: You shall have me assisting you in all.

speaker: GREMIO
text: But will you woo this wildcat?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Will I live?

speaker: GRUMIO
text: Will he woo her? Ay, or I’ll hang her.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Why came I hither but to that intent?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Think you a little din can daunt mine ears?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Have I not in my time heard lions roar?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Have I not heard the sea, puffed up with winds,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Rage like an angry boar chafèd with sweat?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Have I not heard great ordnance in the field

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And heaven’s artillery thunder in the skies?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Have I not in a pitchèd battle heard

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Loud ’larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets clang?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And do you tell me of a woman’s tongue,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That gives not half so great a blow to hear

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As will a chestnut in a farmer’s fire?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Tush, tush, fear boys with bugs!

speaker: GRUMIO
text: For he fears none.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Hortensio, hark.

speaker: GREMIO
text: This gentleman is happily arrived,

speaker: GREMIO
text: My mind presumes, for his own good and yours.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I promised we would be contributors

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And bear his charge of wooing whatsoe’er.

speaker: GREMIO
text: And so we will, provided that he win her.

speaker: GRUMIO
text: I would I were as sure of a good dinner.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Gentlemen, God save you. If I may be bold,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way

speaker: TRANIO
text: To the house of Signior Baptista Minola?

speaker: BIONDELLO
text: He that has the two fair daughters—is ’t

speaker: BIONDELLO
text: he you mean?

speaker: TRANIO
text: Even he, Biondello.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Hark you, sir, you mean not her to—

speaker: TRANIO
text: Perhaps him and her, sir. What have you to do?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.

speaker: TRANIO
text: I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let’s away.

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: Well begun, Tranio.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Sir, a word ere you go.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Are you a suitor to the maid you talk of, yea or no?

speaker: TRANIO
text: An if I be, sir, is it any offense?

speaker: GREMIO
text: No, if without more words you will get you hence.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Why sir, I pray, are not the streets as free

speaker: TRANIO
text: For me, as for you?

speaker: GREMIO
text: But so is not she.

speaker: TRANIO
text: For what reason, I beseech you?

speaker: GREMIO
text: For this reason, if you’ll know:

speaker: GREMIO
text: That she’s the choice love of Signior Gremio.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: That she’s the chosen of Signior Hortensio.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Softly, my masters. If you be gentlemen,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Do me this right: hear me with patience.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Baptista is a noble gentleman

speaker: TRANIO
text: To whom my father is not all unknown,

speaker: TRANIO
text: And were his daughter fairer than she is,

speaker: TRANIO
text: She may more suitors have, and me for one.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Fair Leda’s daughter had a thousand wooers.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Then well one more may fair Bianca have.

speaker: TRANIO
text: And so she shall. Lucentio shall make one,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Though Paris came in hope to speed alone.

speaker: GREMIO
text: What, this gentleman will out-talk us all!

speaker: LUCENTIO
text: Sir, give him head; I know he’ll prove a jade.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Sir, let me be so bold as ask you,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Did you yet ever see Baptista’s daughter?

speaker: TRANIO
text: No, sir, but hear I do that he hath two,

speaker: TRANIO
text: The one as famous for a scolding tongue

speaker: TRANIO
text: As is the other for beauteous modesty.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Sir, sir, the first’s for me; let her go by.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Yea, leave that labor to great Hercules,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And let it be more than Alcides’ twelve.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth:

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Her father keeps from all access of suitors

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And will not promise her to any man

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Until the elder sister first be wed.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: The younger then is free, and not before.

speaker: TRANIO
text: If it be so, sir, that you are the man

speaker: TRANIO
text: Must stead us all, and me amongst the rest,

speaker: TRANIO
text: And if you break the ice and do this feat,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Achieve the elder, set the younger free

speaker: TRANIO
text: For our access, whose hap shall be to have her

speaker: TRANIO
text: Will not so graceless be to be ingrate.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Sir, you say well, and well you do conceive.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And since you do profess to be a suitor,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: You must, as we do, gratify this gentleman,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: To whom we all rest generally beholding.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Sir, I shall not be slack; in sign whereof,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Please you we may contrive this afternoon

speaker: TRANIO
text: And quaff carouses to our mistress’ health,

speaker: TRANIO
text: And do as adversaries do in law,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Strive mightily, but eat and drink as friends.

speaker:
GRUMIO
               
              and
               
              BIONDELLO
text: O excellent motion! Fellows, let’s be gone.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: The motion’s good indeed, and be it so.—

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Petruchio, I shall be your ben venuto.
